title: Do Fossils Belong in Mansions or Museums?
gist: The record $50.13 million sale of the T. rex "Gus" continues a pattern of billionaires like Citadel's Ken Griffin buying scientifically significant dinosaur fossils, a trend the author frames as an embodiment of widening wealth gaps that pulls specimens away from museums and public science.

insights.claim: An unknown buyer paid $50,130,000 for the T. rex skeleton "Gus" at Sotheby's, the largest sum yet paid by a private collector for a vertebrate fossil.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Confirms an escalating price trend for trophy fossils among private buyers.
insights.quote: $50,130,000 to be exact for “Gus” the T. rex.
insights.locator: This morning an as-yet unknown buyer

insights.claim: Citadel founder and CEO Ken Griffin paid more than $44 million in 2024 for the Stegosaurus "Apex," which is currently on loan to the New York Museum of Natural History for two more years.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
entities: New York Museum of Natural History
insights.so_what: Shows billionaire buyers can still return value to public science via loans, a possible model to watch as fossil prices climb.

insights.claim: In 2007 actor Nicolas Cage paid $276,000 for a Tarbosaurus (T. bataar) skull that was later found to be stolen and had to be returned to the Mongolian government.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Illustrates provenance and legal risk in the private fossil market, relevant to buyers and auction houses alike.

insights.claim: The rise of private collectors buying scientifically significant dinosaur fossils embodies the broader trend of widening wealth gaps globally.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Frames record fossil sales as a proxy indicator of concentrated private wealth.

insights.claim: Fossils sold into private hands instead of museums can limit what scientists can learn from them and keep significant specimens away from public view.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: A durable risk for the field: record auction prices may shrink the pool of specimens available for research.

top_entities: New York Museum of Natural History
